channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Looks for a Silver Bullet to End the Iran War. There May Be None.
item.description: The president is trying to ratchet up the economic pressure on Tehran, but Iran’s government is unlikely to make a deal without a big, face-saving compromise.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran last week, depicting a closed Strait of Hormuz over the mouth of President Trump.

item.title: What We Know About ‘Project Freedom’ in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Violence flared in the waterway on Monday after President Trump said the United States would help guide stranded ships. It’s not clear exactly how the plan is working.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Ships in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, last week. Some 1,600 vessels are believed to be stranded.

item.title: Trump Administration Investigating Smith College Over Transgender Admissions
item.description: The Education Department’s civil rights arm said admitting “biological males” to the women’s college may violate anti-discrimination laws.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smith College, one of the nation’s largest single-sex schools with about 2,500 students, has been admitting transgender students since 2015, along with several other top women’s colleges.

item.title: Judge Refers Justice Dept. Lawyer for Possible Discipline, Calling Out ‘Lack of Candor’
item.description: Officials withheld a detainee’s overseas arrest warrant from a federal judge. When she ordered his release, they used the same information to attack her publicly.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: USA Today Network, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: Judge Melissa R. DuBose, left, after being sworn in as a U.S. district judge in Cranston, R.I., last year.

item.title: F.A.A. Proposes Rule for Drone-Free Zones
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration proposed a system for designating certain “critical infrastructure” sites off limits to unmanned aircraft.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Aj Mast/Associated Press
item.media:description: A drone flying over Memorial Stadium in Bloomington, Ind., before a college football game.

item.title: These 7 Primary Elections Will Test President Trump’s Power in Indiana
item.description: The president is seeking political payback for Republican state legislators in Indiana who rejected his push to redraw the state’s political map last year.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Indiana state legislators met in Indianapolis last year before a vote on redrawing the state’s congressional map.

item.title: Paxton’s Fund-Raising Struggles in Texas Underscore Deep Rift in G.O.P.
item.description: The Texas attorney general is heading into the runoff against Senator John Cornyn without the support of some of his longtime backers.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: On the campaign trail for U.S. Senate, the Texas attorney general, Ken Paxton, has presented his fund-raising gap as a strength.

item.title: Democrats Urge N.Y. Leaders to Redistrict After Supreme Court Ruling
item.description: The House minority leader, Hakeem Jeffries, sent a familiar emissary to the New York State Capitol to convey the party’s urgency to draw more favorable maps.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Associated Press
item.media:description: Representative Joseph Morelle, a former New York State legislator, returned to Albany on Tuesday to push Democrats to pass a redistricting measure.

item.title: Ohio’s 2026 Senate Primary Election Kicks Off With Attacks About Epstein
item.description: Ohio’s unusual contest — with a little-known incumbent and a well-known challenger — shows how Democrats are hoping to capitalize on G.O.P. voters’ anger at the Jeffrey Epstein scandal.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times; Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Senator Sherrod Brown of Ohio, left, is running again for the chamber against Senator Jon Husted, a Republican who was appointed to his seat by the governor to replace Vice President JD Vance.

item.title: F.D.A. Blocked Publication of Research Finding Covid and Shingles Vaccines Were Safe
item.description: The agency’s scientists and data contractors reviewed millions of patient records for studies that were pulled back before release.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Covid-19 vaccine distribution site in Salt Lake City in 2022.

item.title: When ‘The Late Show With Stephen Colbert’ Goes Away, What Do We Lose?
item.description: Our attachment to an institution may seem counterintuitive, especially with comedy, a rebellious art form. But with Stephen Colbert’s program, there was a lot at stake.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Celina Pereira; Photographs, via Getty Images

item.title: The Federal Safety Net Isn’t Ready for Artificial Intelligence
item.description: As fears of A.I.-driven job losses mount, economists warn that unemployment benefits and other programs to help displaced workers aren’t sufficient.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Felix Decombat

item.title: U.S.C. Will Infuse A.I. Across University with $200 Million Donation
item.description: The University of Southern California wants to integrate artificial intelligence into fields like health care and the arts. Other universities are also using donations to focus more on A.I.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Southern California campus in Los Angeles.

item.title: Coinbase Lays Off 14% of Employees as A.I. Changes Work
item.description: The largest U.S. crypto exchange said it was cutting jobs because of cryptocurrency market volatility and to “optimize” for the artificial intelligence era.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Gabby Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: Coinbase, which went public in 2021, is cutting 14 percent of its work force.

item.title: Five Publishers and Scott Turow Sue Meta and Mark Zuckerberg
item.description: The class-action lawsuit accuses the tech giant and its founder and chief executive of infringing on authors’ copyrights.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mark Zuckerberg in March 2026.

item.title: Was It Art? Was It Fashion? Was It Good?
item.description: Guests at the Met Gala had different interpretations of the night’s dress code.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kim Kardashian, in an orange fiberglass breastplate by the British pop artist Allen Jones.

item.title: Inside Meatstock, the Carnivore Diet Convention Where Steak Is Medicine
item.description: Devotees of the diet, which Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. follows, bonded over brisket and butter at Meatstock.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum and Juan Diego Reyes for The New York Times
item.media:credit: Juan Diego Reyes for The New York Times

item.title: Modi’s Triumph in West Bengal Elections Puts Him Closer to an Opposition-Free India
item.description: With his triumph in West Bengal state elections, Prime Minister Narendra Modi has moved closer to his dream of an opposition-free India.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Hari Kumar and Pragati K.B.
item.media:credit: Sahiba Chawdhary/Reuters
item.media:description: Supporters of Mr. Modi’s Bharatiya Janata Party celebrating in Kolkata as the party headed to victory in the West Bengal state assembly elections on Monday.

item.title: James Murdoch’s Company Said to Be in Talks to Acquire Major Parts of Vox Media
item.description: A deal for Vox Media, which generates more than $80 million through podcasts alone, would elevate Mr. Murdoch’s company, Lupa Systems, as a major player in U.S. media.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Jessica Testa
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Murdoch, the younger son of the media mogul Rupert Murdoch, in Washington, D.C., in 2020.

item.title: The FBI Searched a Washington Post Reporter’s Home. She Is Now a Pulitzer Prize Winner.
item.description: Hannah Natanson’s reporting anchored a package of articles from The Washington Post that won a Pulitzer on Monday, four months after agents seized her devices.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner, via, via Reuters
item.media:description: Hannah Natanson, a reporter and member of the winning team for the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service, in the Washington Post newsroom on Monday.

item.title: With Mamdani’s Rise, Muslim Democrats Become Power Players
item.description: The Muslim Democratic Club was founded to increase political clout among the city’s Muslims. Now some of its founders are the mayor’s top aides.
item.dc:creator: Maya King
item.category: Muslim Democratic Club of New York
item.media:credit: Camille Farrah Lenain for The New York Times
item.media:description: Faiza Ali, one of the founding members of the Muslim Democratic Club, is now commissioner of the Mayor’s Office of Immigrant Affairs in New York City.

item.title: In Attack on Mamdani, Vornado Chief Likens ‘Tax the Rich’ to Hate Speech
item.description: Steven Roth, the chief executive of Vornado, criticized Mayor Zohran Mamdani for singling out the billionaire Ken Griffin in his bid to make the wealthy pay more taxes in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Misha Friedman/Getty Images
item.media:description: Steven Roth, the chief executive of Vornado Realty Trust, likened “tax the rich” to hate speech.

item.title: N.Y.P.D. Captain Is Transferred After Calling Mamdani ‘Total Nonsense’
item.description: The captain, James G. Wilson, was also filmed calling Democrats a “waste of human race.”
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer and Andy Newman
item.media:credit: Mary Altaffer/Associated Press

item.title: New Musicals ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Lost Boys’ Lead List of Tony Nominees
item.description: The nominated performers include the film stars Daniel Radcliffe, Rose Byrne, John Lithgow and Lesley Manville.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Schmigadoon!” is adapted from a comedic Apple TV series and received 12 Tony nominations, tied with “The Lost Boys” for the most.

item.title: Tony Awards Nominations 2026: The Complete List
item.description: Nominations for the 79th Tony Awards were announced on Tuesday. Here’s who made the list.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: A scene from “The Lost Boys,” which earned 12 Tony nominations including best new musical.

item.title: Tony Nominations Snubs and Surprises: Lea Michele Left Out, ‘Titaníque’ Shines
item.description: Adrien Brody, Jean Smart and other boldface names were left out, while June Squibb, André De Shields and Layton Williams as an iceberg were among the surprises.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Jesse Green, Alexis Soloski, Scott Heller, Elisabeth Vincentelli and Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Photographs by Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Clockwise from top left, Lea Michele and Nicholas Christopher in “Chess”; Adrien Brody in “The Fear of 13”; Rachel Dratch in “The Rocky Horror Show”; and André De Shields in “Cats: The Jellicle Ball.”

item.title: Cruise Ship Struck by Hantavirus Is to Head to Canary Islands, W.H.O. Says
item.description: Spain said it would decide on a port of call only after disease experts inspected the vessel. The World Health Organization said human-to-human transmission may have played a role in the outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim, Lynsey Chutel and Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Arilson Almeida/Associated Press
item.media:description: The MV Hondius moored off the coast of Cape Verde, an archipelago in the Atlantic Ocean, on Monday.

item.title: What to Know About the Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: Health authorities said three passengers from the MV Hondius had died after showing symptoms of the rare disease, with another critically ill and three more showing mild symptoms.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: A Democrat Who Makes Me Listen
item.description: An interview with Representative Jake Auchincloss, a Democrat from the charismatic center.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.media:credit: Greg Kahn for The New York Times

item.title: This Trump Proposal is Good for Private Equity — and Not Good for Our Retirement Accounts
item.description: A win for private equity. A loss for our retirement assets.
item.dc:creator: Steven Rattner

item.title: U.S. Sues The New York Times, Claiming Discrimination Against a White Man
item.description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission said the paper had engaged in “unlawful employment practices” against the man, who did not get a sought-after promotion.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission has been investigating a discrimination claim filed last year against The New York Times by a white male employee.

item.title: A Climate Activist Wants to Run California. Coal Helped Fuel His Wealth.
item.description: Questions linger about the finances of Tom Steyer, who has emerged as a leading contender in the California governor’s race. He says he has asked that his money be separated from fossil fuel investments.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim, Julie Tate and Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Fred Greaves/Reuters
item.media:description: Tom Steyer has become a leading contender in the California governor’s race since the sudden withdrawal of Eric Swalwell last month.

item.title: Delta Air Lines Will Stop Serving Snacks and Drinks on Short Flights
item.description: Most travelers flying less than 350 miles on Delta will soon have to go without free coffee and cookies, but the carrier is adding service to longer flights.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:credit: Stephen Maturen/Getty Images
item.media:description: About 9 percent of Delta’s daily flights will no longer have free snack and beverage service after May 19.

item.title: How to Catch a Meteor Shower From Halley’s Comet
item.description: Known for their speed, the Eta Aquarids will reach their peak on Tuesday night into Wednesday morning.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: W. Liller/NASA
item.media:description: Halley’s comet over Easter Island in 1986. The Eta Aquarids meteor shower is the result of debris from Halley’s tail.
